title: DIY NanoDrop Microvolume Spectrophotometer Protocol for Low-Resource Labs
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"faol0","text":"This protocol outlines the construction and operation of a DIY NanoDrop spectrophotometer for quantifying DNA at 260 nm - 280nm, utilizing a USB webcam, UV- LED, DVD diffraction grating, mirror, and blackbox enclosure. It includes step-by-step instructions for assembly, calibration, and measurement, tailored for use with Theremino Spectrometer software. Suitable for educational or low-budget laboratory settings.","type":"align-justify","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"2q7b1","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}

wipes","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"4j25q","text":"• UV-protective software","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"clbs6","text":"• DNA sample (e.g., 238.5 ng/ul ladder marker DNA)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: Step 1: Constructing the Blackbox Enclosure 1. Cut plywood/acrylic into panels: 2x (20 cm x 15 cm), 2x (20 cm x 10 cm), 2x (15 cm x 10 cm). ( this file provides the laser cutter dimensions, but you could increase size to contain the camera you use) 2. Glue panels to form a light-tight box, keeping the side panel detachable for access. 3. Line the interior with black velvet or Black 2.0 paint, ensuring no reflections. plywood laser cut parts

Step 2: Preparing the DVD Diffraction Grating 1. Split a DVD-R, remove silvering/dye with rubbing alcohol, and cut to 3 cm x 3 cm. 2. Mount the grating at a 45 ℃ angle inside the enclosure, almost 7 cm from the sample holder, using glue or a holder. DVD-R as a diffraction grating
Step 3: Creating the Optical Slit 1. Form a 1 mm slit using black tape or razor blade. 2. Attach to the front panel, aligning with the UV- LED position.

Step 4: Installing the UV- LED 1. Mount the 260 nm LED on the front panel that has a hinge in the back, make the LED aligned with the slit. 2. Wire the LED to a switch, switch to 9v battery with the resistor; wired DIY nanodrop device, the 9v battery in the back not visible.

Step 5: Assembling the Sample Holder 1. Mount a stand pedestal with UV-transparent surfaces like microscope slides. 2. Adjust the gap to exactly 1x1 mm using a ruler, aligned with the upper pedestal to a hinged arm. 3. Clean pedestal with deionized water and lint-free wipes before use.
Step 6: Mounting the Mirror 1. Place the first-surface mirror at a 45 ℃ angle to reflect light to the webcam. 2. Secure with glue or a holder.

Step 7: Installing the Webcam 1. Remove the IR filter from the USB webcam for UV sensitivity. (some webcams would break if you do this, you could do a simple calibration for it) 2. Mount on the back panel, aligned with the reflected light path. 3. Connect to a computer via USB for Theremino integration. webcam as sensor
Step 8: Wiring the Circuit 1. Connect: 9V battery to the UV LED (use a resistor of 120 Ω). 2. Insulate all connections. 3. Ensure correct LED polarity to avoid damage.

Step 9: Aligning Components 1. Use a laser pointer to align the UV-C LED, slit, sample holder, DVD grating, mirror, and webcam. 2. Ensure the 260 nm light path is unobstructed and reaches the webcam sensor. (this takes 02:00:00 ) assembled DIY nanodrop from the inside

Step 10: Calibrating the Device 1. Seal the enclosure to prevent light leaks. 2. Run Theremino Spectrometer, select the webcam, and calibrate wavelength using a fluorescence lamp (436 nm, 546 nm peaks) or you could use a laser pointer it usually has 630-660 nm range. 3. Pipette 3ul of distilled water onto the sample holder, lower the arm, and set the blank to zero absorbance at 260 nm, 230 nm, and 280 nm.

Step 11: Operating the Device 1. Pipette 3ul of DNA sample (e.g., 238.5 ng/uL DNA) onto the lower pedestal. 2. Lower the hinged arm to form a liquid column, avoiding bubbles. 3. Record the absorbance spectrum in Theremino, focusing on 260 nm. Click save data file to get the range you want with transmttance values against 260 wv. 4. Calculate concentration: c = A260 x50 x 0.1 (in ng/ul). 5. Check purity ratios: A260/A280 (it should be between 1.8 - 2) 6. Clean pedestals with deionized water and wipes between samples. operation of the DIY nanodrop device

Expected Results For a 238.5 ng/uL DNA ladder sample: • Absorbance at 260 nm: 1.19 • Transmittance at 260 nm: 6.46% • Concentration (calculated): 238.5 ng/uL • Purity ratios: A260/280 1.8 The actual transmittance result was in average 6.25% giving an acceptable rate of mistake in 3.25% deviation, within ±5% tolerance. (the process take 00:05:00 ) Example of results output - click save data file to get results in a txt file or table.
